name: One-Sentence Answer Drill
description: Practice answering under pressure with maximum economy. Every answer must be one sentence.
instructions: You will be given 6 cross-examination questions. Answer each in exactly one sentence. No qualifications, no preamble. If you need to qualify, fold it into the sentence.
questions: Does your position apply universally or only in specific conditions?
What is the single most important word in your core claim?
If you could only keep one argument, which one would it be and why?
What is the one thing your opponent must concede for you to win?
Name one real-world case where your position clearly holds.
What is the fastest way someone could refute your entire position?
rubric: Each answer is scored: 0 if more than one sentence, 1 if one sentence but evasive, 2 if one sentence and directly responsive.
completionPrompt: Review your answers. For any that were evasive, rewrite them with a direct commitment.

name: Impact Weighing Drill
linkedWeakness: Vague weighing without magnitude or timeframe
description: Practice comparing two competing impacts using a structured template. Forces you to assign magnitude, probability, and timeframe.
instructions: For each scenario, write one impact for each side. Then compare them using: (1) Magnitude — how severe, (2) Probability — how likely, (3) Timeframe — how soon, (4) Reversibility — can it be undone. End with a one-sentence verdict.
questions: Policy A saves 100 lives immediately. Policy B prevents 10,000 deaths over 20 years but may not work. Which do you fund?
Rule X protects individual rights but makes collective coordination 30% harder. Rule Y does the reverse. Which do you implement?
Certain harm to one person vs. uncertain harm to many. What probability threshold changes your answer?
A policy has a 90% chance of modest improvement and a 10% chance of catastrophic failure. Do you implement it?
rubric: Score 0-3 per scenario: 0 = no weighing, 1 = one dimension only, 2 = two dimensions, 3 = all four dimensions with verdict.
completionPrompt: Go back to your weakest comparison. Add the missing dimensions and rewrite the verdict.

name: Definition Stress Test
description: Build a definition that survives edge cases. You will define a term, then test it against five hard cases.
instructions: Write a definition of the key term in your chosen position. It must be one sentence with a clear boundary rule. Then for each edge case below, answer: does your definition include or exclude this case, and why?
questions: Write your definition here.
Edge case 1: A case that seems clearly inside your definition.
Edge case 2: A case that seems clearly outside your definition.
Edge case 3: A borderline case — which side of the line does it fall on?
Edge case 4: A case your opponent would use to break your definition.
Edge case 5: Rewrite your definition if any edge case revealed a flaw.
rubric: 3 points if the final definition handles all five cases without contradiction. Deduct 1 point per case where the boundary is unclear.
completionPrompt: Show your final definition to someone and ask them to find one more case that breaks it.

name: Concession Control Drill
linkedWeakness: Refusing to concede anything, or conceding too much
description: Practice the art of the strategic concession: give up what you cannot defend while keeping what you need to win.
instructions: For each attack on your position, decide whether to (A) concede fully, (B) concede partially with a condition, or (C) reject entirely. Write your response and explain what you protected.
questions: Your opponent says: 'Your position has exceptions. Name one and I will concede everything else.'
Your opponent says: 'Even your own examples show this does not always work.'
Your opponent says: 'The evidence for your strongest claim is disputed. Admit it.'
Your opponent says: 'Your definition includes a case you clearly do not want to defend.'
Your opponent says: 'You have not addressed the strongest version of my argument.'
rubric: 2 points per response: 1 for making a clear choice (A/B/C), 1 for successfully protecting the core claim.
completionPrompt: Find the response where you gave up the most. Rewrite it as a partial concession that keeps your position intact.

name: Incentive Check Drill
linkedWeakness: Plans that ignore how real people will respond
description: Take any policy position and stress-test it against real human incentives. The goal is to find what breaks it before your opponent does.
instructions: For the policy you are defending, answer each question as concretely as possible. Avoid 'people should' reasoning.
questions: Who benefits from this policy succeeding? What will they do to ensure it does?
Who is harmed by this policy? What will they do to resist or work around it?
What does the average person, acting in self-interest, do under this policy?
What happens when a bad actor gets access to this policy's mechanisms?
What is the single most likely unintended consequence?
rubric: 2 points per answer: 1 for naming a specific actor, 1 for describing a specific behavior (not just an intention).
completionPrompt: Take your worst-case answer and build a safeguard into your position that addresses it.

name: Warrant Bridge Drill
linkedWeakness: Claims with reasons but no logical bridge
description: Every argument has three parts: a claim, a reason, and a warrant (the bridge that connects them). This drill forces you to make all three explicit.
instructions: For each incomplete argument below, write the missing warrant. The warrant explains WHY the reason makes the claim more likely — not just that it does.
questions: Claim: We should implement this policy. Reason: Other countries have done it. Warrant: ???
Claim: This is morally wrong. Reason: Most people would disapprove. Warrant: ???
Claim: The plan will fail. Reason: It has been tried before. Warrant: ???
Claim: Rights should be protected absolutely. Reason: Rights are fundamental. Warrant: ???
Now write a complete three-part argument for your own position in this format: Claim / Reason / Warrant.
rubric: 2 points per warrant: 1 if it connects reason to claim, 1 if it is non-circular (does not repeat the claim).
completionPrompt: Go back to your first three warrants. Can any of them be attacked? Write one line rebutting each.

name: Burden of Proof Drill
linkedWeakness: Unclear about what must be proved to win
description: Winning a debate often comes down to who sets the burden of proof and how high they set it. This drill trains you to control that frame.
instructions: Answer each question for your position in one to two sentences. Be precise about what you must prove and what you do not have to prove.
questions: State exactly what you need to demonstrate to win this debate.
State what you do NOT need to prove (and why the opponent cannot require it).
What is the minimum standard of evidence required for your core claim?
What would count as a loss for your side — name the exact condition.
Rewrite your burden of proof statement so your opponent cannot shift it.
rubric: 2 points per answer: 1 for specificity, 1 for correctly scoping the burden (not too wide, not too narrow).
completionPrompt: Read your burden statement to someone. Ask them: 'Does this feel too easy to meet or too hard?' Adjust based on their answer.

name: Second-Order Consequences Drill
linkedWeakness: First-order thinking that misses downstream effects
description: Good debaters think one move ahead. Great debaters think three. This drill trains second and third-order consequence mapping.
instructions: Start with your policy. For each level, describe what happens next. Be specific — name who acts, what they do, and what changes as a result.
questions: First-order: What is the direct, intended effect of this policy?
Second-order: What do affected parties do in response to that first effect?
Third-order: What happens to the system after those responses?
Negative path: Walk the chain to the worst plausible outcome.
Positive path: Walk the chain to the best realistic outcome.
rubric: 2 points per level: 1 for naming a specific actor or mechanism, 1 for describing a plausible causal chain.
completionPrompt: Use your third-order analysis to add one safeguard or caveat to your original position.

name: Steelman Drill
linkedWeakness: Attacking weak versions of opponent arguments
description: You cannot beat an argument you have not understood. This drill forces you to build the strongest version of the opposing view — stronger than your opponent built it.
instructions: Build the strongest possible version of the position you are arguing against. Do not include any weaknesses. Then explain why you still disagree.
questions: Write the opposing position's single best argument in two sentences.
What evidence would most strongly support that argument?
What moral intuition does that argument appeal to?
Write the version of this argument that would be hardest for you to answer.
Now explain, in two sentences, why you still disagree with this steelmanned version.
rubric: 2 points per answer: 1 for genuine engagement with the opposing view, 1 for accuracy (would the opponent recognize their argument?).
completionPrompt: Show your steelman to someone who holds the opposing view. Ask: 'Is this a fair representation?' Revise if not.

name: Summary Crystallization Drill
linkedWeakness: Weak summary speeches that do not collapse to clear voters
description: A summary speech should do three things: collapse the debate to two voters, explain why you win each one, and tell the judge why it matters. This drill builds that skill.
instructions: After completing a debate session, write a summary speech using exactly this structure. Each section has a strict word limit.
questions: Voter 1 (name it in 5 words or fewer): Why does this argument matter most?
Why do you win Voter 1? (2 sentences max)
Voter 2 (name it in 5 words or fewer): Why does this argument matter second?
Why do you win Voter 2? (2 sentences max)
Decision rule: Complete this sentence — 'If you believe X, vote for my side because Y.'
rubric: 3 points per voter (name + win explanation + connection to evidence), 4 points for a decision rule that is clear and non-circular.
completionPrompt: Read your summary aloud. If it takes more than 60 seconds, cut it until it fits.
